title: Stakeholder Models
summary: Three ways to classify stakeholders by influence — the Power/Interest grid, the Salience model, and the Stakeholder Cube — and how to choose between them.

sections.heading: Why classify stakeholders
sections.body:
You can't engage everyone the same way, so stakeholder analysis sorts people by how much they can affect the project and how affected they are by it. The output drives your engagement plan: who to manage closely, who to keep satisfied, who to keep informed, and who to simply monitor.

Three models do this at increasing levels of detail:

- Power/Interest grid — two dimensions, a 2x2 quadrant. The everyday default.
- Salience model — three dimensions (power, legitimacy, urgency) shown as a Venn diagram.
- Stakeholder Cube — three dimensions turned into a 3D cube, adding attitude.

All three are classification tools used during stakeholder identification and analysis. None is 'better' — they trade simplicity for nuance.
sections.tip: Classification is never one-and-done. People move between categories as the project, their interest, and their urgency change — re-map at major milestones.

sections.heading: Power/Interest grid
sections.body:
The most common model. It plots each stakeholder on two axes — power (their ability to influence the outcome) and interest (how much they care about it) — creating four quadrants, each with a standard engagement strategy:

- High power, high interest — Manage closely: your key players; involve them and meet their expectations.
- High power, low interest — Keep satisfied: enough attention that they don't become blockers, but don't overload them.
- Low power, high interest — Keep informed: engaged supporters and the 'affected'; communicate regularly, they can be useful allies.
- Low power, low interest — Monitor: minimal effort, but watch for movement.

Close relatives swap one axis: the Power/Influence grid uses active involvement instead of interest, and the Influence/Impact grid uses ability to change the plan.
sections.tip: Memorise the quadrants by power first: high power = Manage Closely (also interested) or Keep Satisfied (not). Low power = Keep Informed (interested) or Monitor (not).

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='44' y1='16' x2='44' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='44' y1='172' x2='306' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='175' y1='16' x2='175' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='44' y1='94' x2='306' y2='94' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><text x='109' y='58' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Keep Satisfied</text><text x='240' y='58' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Manage Closely</text><text x='109' y='136' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Monitor</text><text x='240' y='136' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Keep Informed</text><text x='26' y='94' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 26 94)'>Power</text><text x='175' y='195' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Interest</text></svg>
figure.caption: Power up the side, interest across the bottom; each quadrant has its own strategy.

sections.heading: Salience model
sections.body:
The salience model adds a third dimension. It scores each stakeholder on three attributes and uses the overlaps of a Venn diagram to set priority — the more attributes someone holds, the more 'salient' (prominent) they are.

- Power — can they influence the outcome or withhold resources?
- Legitimacy — is their involvement proper and authorised (a sponsor, owner, regulator)?
- Urgency — how time-critical is their claim, how fast do they need a response?

The overlaps create seven classes, conveniently all 'D' words:

- One attribute (latent):
  - Dormant (power only).
  - Discretionary (legitimacy only).
  - Demanding (urgency only).
- Two attributes (expectant):
  - Dominant (power + legitimacy).
  - Dependent (legitimacy + urgency — no power, so they need a champion or coalition).
  - Dangerous (power + urgency, no legitimacy — coercive, may bypass process).
- All three (definitive):
  - Definitive — the centre of the diagram, your highest priority.

Engagement follows salience: manage Definitive and Dependent closely, keep Dangerous satisfied to contain the threat, keep Dominant and Demanding informed, and monitor Dormant and Discretionary.
sections.tip: Urgency is the volatile one — it spikes with deadlines and crises. A Discretionary or Dependent stakeholder can jump to Definitive overnight, so re-score regularly.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 212' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='125' cy='86' r='62' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='197' cy='86' r='62' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='161' cy='146' r='62' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='66' y='40' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Power</text><text x='256' y='40' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Legitimacy</text><text x='161' y='208' fill='#B5462F' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Urgency</text><text x='88' y='80' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dormant</text><text x='234' y='80' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Discretionary</text><text x='161' y='184' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Demanding</text><text x='161' y='56' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dominant</text><text x='112' y='130' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dangerous</text><text x='210' y='130' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dependent</text><text x='161' y='103' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Definitive</text></svg>
figure.caption: Three attributes overlap; a stakeholder with all three (Definitive) is highest priority.

sections.heading: Stakeholder Cube
sections.body:
The cube takes the 2D Power/Interest grid and extrudes it into three dimensions, adding attitude (supportive 'backer' vs. opposed 'blocker'). Combining power (high/low), interest (active/passive), and attitude (backer/blocker) gives eight stakeholder 'characters', each a corner of the cube:

- Saviour — high power, active, backer: your best ally; engage fully.
- Saboteur — high power, active, blocker: the biggest threat; manage and contain.
- Sleeping giant — high power, passive, backer: wake them up — a powerful supporter not yet engaged.
- Time bomb — high power, passive, blocker: dormant opposition that could detonate; watch closely.
- Friend — low power, active, backer: a confidant and sounding board.
- Trip wire — low power, active, blocker: minor but vocal opposition; can trip you up.
- Acquaintance — low power, passive, backer: keep gently informed.
- Irritant — low power, passive, blocker: low impact; don't over-invest.

The cube's value is that it makes attitude explicit — two stakeholders with identical power and interest may need opposite handling depending on whether they back or block you.
sections.tip: The cube is the most detailed but least commonly tested. Know that it adds attitude (backer vs. blocker) to power and interest, and that a high-power blocker (Saboteur / Time bomb) is the corner to fear.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 212' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><polyline points='126,150 246,150 246,30 126,30 126,150' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4 3' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='66' y1='188' x2='126' y2='150' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='186' y1='188' x2='246' y2='150' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='186' y1='68' x2='246' y2='30' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='66' y1='68' x2='126' y2='30' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><polyline points='66,188 186,188 186,68 66,68 66,188' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.6'/><text x='40' y='128' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 40 128)'>Power</text><text x='126' y='205' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Interest</text><text x='216' y='52' fill='currentColor' font-size='10'>Attitude</text><text x='190' y='64' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='8' text-anchor='start'>Saviour</text><text x='58' y='64' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='end'>Saboteur</text></svg>
figure.caption: Power x interest x attitude — eight corners, from Saviour (high power, active, backer) to Irritant.

sections.heading: Choosing a model
sections.body:
All three are valid PMI classification models — pick the one that fits the situation:

- Power/Interest grid — fast, intuitive, good for most projects and for communicating with leadership.
- Salience model — when timing and legitimacy matter, e.g. regulators, crisis response, or stakeholders whose urgency swings.
- Stakeholder Cube — when attitude is decisive and you need to separate supporters from opponents who otherwise look identical.

Whatever you use, classification feeds the stakeholder register and the engagement plan, and it is a living judgement, not a one-time label.
sections.tip: Exam clue: 'power, legitimacy, and urgency' = salience model. 'Power and interest' = the grid. Adding 'attitude' or backer/blocker = the cube.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam. Salience model attributes per PMBOK Guide; stakeholder cube per Murray-Webster & Simon.
